speaker: interviewer
text: Number two in the interview with Edward Polidi. Before you describe leaving the, the gypsy quarters, the ghetto, can you-- to the best of your recollection-- describe that year that you spent in this area, in this ghettoed area?

speaker: interviewee
text: Basically, I was staying at home, learning. With my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would he teach you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was studying some Hebrew words, the letters of the Hebrew alphabet, basic mathematics-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there an option of school?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the beginning was some school in the synagogue. But later on, I think that this disappear. Was question of less than half year, in which I, of course, I was included there. And there, we were studying practically how to, to write and read the local language.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bulgarian and Hebrew letters.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language did you speak at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: At home, we were speaking Bulgarian language. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Of course, my grandparents were speaking some kind of, for me, unknown language that I tried to understand and communicate. Later on, I, I were able to understand the meaning of some words. And this language was called Ladino, which is the-- actually, this language that the Spanish Jews, the Jews that came from Spain, used to speak between them and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Does it resemble another language?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, resemble very much to the older Spain, older Latin language, perhaps from-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] for this reason, the semantics of this language remain in the name of the language, Ladino, which is Latin language. So this was the language that also my parents were speaking between themselves. I didn't study formally this language. But how happen usually because of the curiosity of the little children, anytime when they hear something, some language that is spoken by the parents, they start almost intuitively to learn and understand. So this, perhaps, later on helped me when I was in my own traveling in the world very quickly absorbed other languages, specially with Latin roots. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But of course, the official language to us a Bulgarian language, which they spoke on a very academic level, a very high literary level. This what was basically with the Jewish population of, of the Sofia, this what is remarkable that I, I never heard [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the Bulgarian language spoken of them that is not absolutely correct, precise, and in, in very high level language. I never heard Jewish people there speaking incorrectly the, the language, never heard Jewish people speak with the slangs that you very often will find in different areas or in different classes of the country.

speaker: interviewer
text: So [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] perhaps this was one of the reasons why, actually between the elite around the king, most of the intellectuals, most of the very big businessmen, were of Jewish descendants, was Jewish people. Perhaps also this was one of the reasons of this basic sympathies of the king of Bulgaria towards the Jewish people. And this is what perhaps saved the life of majority of people for difference of Greek Jewish populations or other neighboring countries that were transported to the German deadly camps, like Romania, for instance, or Yugoslavia, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] or Hungary. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So one person was able there to feel actually very strong the, the Jewish community. Maybe this was after-- the reason why, between the children, was appearing this kind of very childish negative attitude, since the fascistic classes used to give to the Bulgarian children, specially of little bit poorer classes, clothing, special clothing. It will formal clothing, brownish, with some of them with the swastika. And some of them even used to carry a, a knife, special military knife.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, and they were treated almost as a favorite youths of this certain circles of Bulgarian political circles. They used to call them [NON-ENGLISH] meaning kind of children or youths that is faithful to the Bulgarian people and the, the governments.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember seeing children dressed this way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I experience [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] personally some of the activity of some of these little children. I was very little-- first of all, with this little accident that happened when I was approaching my fourth year, three and a half, fourth years, when they used to throw all my eyes sand. Later on, I, I was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] number of times maltreated and, and even beaten of these children. But fortunately enough, this didn't happen too often. And we, we left this area.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe one of those incidents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, was once when I was leaving the, the school there in the synagogue. And with the curiosity of a young child, I decided to see what is happening in the neighboring streets there. And when I arrived to-- or I should say immediately before that, that the Jewish children were able also to be distinguished by their dresses also, very poor dressed. And they were supposed to dress in certain way. And they were also-- the Jewish people was obliged to carry yellow six corner star.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you wear one?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think that I should be wearing one, yes. I don't recall very exactly. I know-- remember that my parents were, were, were wearing. But I remember very distinctly that when I met some of these children, they start to run after me. They were older than me. And eventually, they cornered me in some place before that I be able to reach back. And this is where I had the bloody nose and, and black eyes, and some-- lot of blue signs in my back.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would your parents react when this happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: They accuse me. I received also in the time very strong warning of my father that I never should leave the area that I supposed to do that. And if it wasn't my mother to protect me and with, with tears, perhaps I would be very well beaten by my father with slaps. Anyway, what happened after that, I received my good lesson, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] this without realizing that I was carrying the lessons of so many Jewish people there. This-- of course, I never after that didn't try to make adventure. And if something happened, this happened in different ways [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and later on. So we, at certain point, had to leave the capital on the trains going to our destination to new location outside of the capital in Vidin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you left there, you were going to describe for me the area, the gypsy quarters as you called them. You stayed there for one year.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe what you remember about that time, what it was like to live there?

speaker: interviewee
text: My hobby, if I'm able to say that, when I was alone at home was basically to study what my assignment was and to play music, actually. This is when I started very actively as one of my favorite time to spend on my few instruments that I had there and start to compose tunes. I recall in this time also that once my uncle surprised me-- brother, older brother, of my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it my activity, musical activity. And he introduced to me scales, how I am able to produce actually diatonic scale and some of the instrument, the xylophone as well as the recorder. So this somewhat developed my techniques of playing and increase my musical knowledge. I also, on the time, seems to me that received my first unformal lesson of music how to write and how to read these little scales that were introduced to me. Do you remember the living conditions?

speaker: interviewee
text: Other conditions-- basically, the mood of the people was very depress. All this visiting and festive dresses disappear.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were your parents still working?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think that my father, he was not working anymore. He was spending--

speaker: interviewer
text: And he has been doing what before you moved into this area?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was, before that, teaching mathematics in the universities, and physics. He-- I think that what they basically were doing were arranging their activities in the synagogue and [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] trying to develop some kind of survival. How they arranged the survival and bringing the food, this was little but unknown terrain for me. But food was always at home. We had all the time what to eat. And basically, we were kept in-- I was kept in good health.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you allowed to leave that area?

speaker: interviewee
text: Formerly, I think that I was officially allowed to leave this area, regarding the laws of the government. But the factum, I was not allowed to leave. Because any Jewish person that was living there was subject to some very severe, very ferocious persecution, being beaten or risk to be killed, even.

speaker: interviewer
text: If they left the area?

speaker: interviewee
text: If they left the area. So the really official persecutions came little bit later on after that we left the city. And obviously, the, the government establish much more their character as a fascistic government. This was the next step in the thing-- in which the thing become even harder, grimmer, and darker. We lefted the city in train during the night, travel quite a long time in this very primitive trains, moving extremely slow.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: With the horse or for animals transportation, wagons that were for transportation through of animals. This was starting in Sofia, going north in a different direction. And they-- during the night-- they used to, at certain location, to take also different directions the wagons, reconnected to a different trains going in different direction, always in north of Sofia, and most of them aimed to toward the cities or villages that were on the river Danube, obviously for easier transportation there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were you with?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was with my mother and my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was the year early 1941, '41. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] After, I understood that some of my cousins were also sended with the same three. Of course, took a long time before that I saw them already when we arrive on this village, Vidin, that this--

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you on the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were perhaps three or four days there. A long time took this waiting for relocation of the trains and somewhere in the middle of the route. So because the distance-- actually, there were not so great distances. They are pretty short distances. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you see outside of the train? Do you remember passing through areas?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, while possible to see if you are close to the door and the door is open, which was not very often. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But there was not so much what to see in the time. Because basically, was winter season, cold, snow. And one person, what my mother always was trying that I and many mothers other, to preserve that the children don't become so sick that they die. Some, some of the children got sick there. It's true. I don't know who died or not. But the conditions were horrible, awful. I don't have too many memories on this trip, besides the fact that I wanted soon as possible to finish with that, was kind of suffocating experience. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When we arrived there, first of all, we were accompanied by soldiers that brought us to some old school, school premises, or other buildings that were in public services. I [INAUDIBLE] don't exactly what kind of services there were.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they--

speaker: interviewee
text: But I remember that we used to sleep in the floor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the soldiers Bulgarian soldiers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bulgarian soldiers, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: They took you to a school?

speaker: interviewee
text: We used to sleep, yeah, in very big rooms, very big something like shelter with many people also sleeping there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the-- were the families separated?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the beginning, the family was not separate. Later on, this happened. But we did remain there for a extremely long time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were there-- in Vidin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Vidin?

speaker: interviewee
text: Vidin, yes. This city is located in the corner between Bulgaria, Romanian the other side of Danube, and Yugoslavia. So this was the northwest corner of Bulgaria, [INAUDIBLE], the northwestern corner. Wasn't not very big city with old, ancient history, by the way. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There were remnants, historic remnants, of a ancient Bulgarians there, even some remnants of [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] battles that were going in the-- before middle centuries. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So the life started there. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Very soon, we find out that we have some cousins that were [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] transported also on the same location. And one of those was my uncle that was older brother of my mother. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Later on, obviously, they found some other people. Because they found some kind of relatives that succeed somehow to find some little more decent place for my, my parents to live, take them out of this shelter, common shelter in which all this people is to live. I remember that we had some kind of little house of one and a half room that was, obviously, inhabited of some very poor people or gypsies of the town there, which my mother somehow succeed to clean it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You lived with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we used to live only with my father and my mother in this house, so called-- one small, small, small house of one, little more than one room.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know how your parents obtained this house, these quarters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Obviously through some connections, relatives, and perhaps my mother giving some of her intimate jewelry there. I don't know exactly what the deal was there. But I remember that tree we started to live little bit differently. And my mother already was able to cook. Of course, the, the life there was not much, much different than the, the life of this shelter. First of all, was not very far. Or the shelter was also very close. And she remain in connection with number of people that are living there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is how they-- perhaps my parents were [INAUDIBLE] of what was happening with the life, the destiny of other Jewish people passing through within and disappearing. Some of them were Jewish people coming from Greece, from northern Greece, like Thessaloniki, some of them from, perhaps, Athens. Were quite big groups of people in situation, perhaps, worst. Because I remember that they were begging for food, some food. Obviously, they traveled much longer. And I don't know if they had food during this travel that sometime were, were going weeks, maybe months.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were just in transit through Vidin?

speaker: interviewee
text: In transit, yes. And perhaps some of them were deprived of food for weeks. Who knows how many of them died in travelling there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember seeing them or hearing stories?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember seeing them. Because [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my mother used to bring them-- bring me with her when she was preparing some kind of food for some of these people with other women of the shelter there, other Jewish women, that they, between them, they were saving from their meager foods in order to give to these people there that were dying of food-- very weak, very [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] desperately-looking people. I-- this what stick of my mind in the, the time as it was. In one of these gatherings, I was ask to play, to bring my musical instruments and perform.

speaker: interviewer
text: For these--

speaker: interviewee
text: For this--

speaker: interviewer
text: These people in transit?

speaker: interviewee
text: People, yes. So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I never will forget this amazing impression that I saw in the faces of this people when I was performing, specially after the time I was finishing my songs, to perform for them. Obviously, my performance was touching very deep portions of their hearts, these deep strings there. Because I was seeing tears in their eyes.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry. We have to break for the next tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry. I know you--
